required.title: [For Mentor] The Whole-Brain Child
required.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

required.title: [For Mentor] No-Drama Discipline
required.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories featuring characters who experience strong emotions and choose their responses

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] When Sophie Gets Angry — Really, Really Angry
suggested.author: Molly Bang

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Alexander and the Terrible, Horrible, No Good, Very Bad Day
suggested.author: Judith Viorst

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Raising an Emotionally Intelligent Child
supplementary.author: John Gottman

competence_markers.description: They can name their emotions with specificity beyond "mad," "sad," and "happy" — using words like frustrated, disappointed, embarrassed, worried, overwhelmed
They demonstrate a pause between feeling and action in at least some emotional moments — not every time, but with increasing frequency
They use words to express feelings instead of defaulting to physical outbursts (hitting, throwing, screaming) — the trend is toward verbal expression
They can identify that a feeling passed — they remember being very upset and can recognize they are no longer upset; the concept of feelings as temporary is understood
They engage in post-event reflection honestly — they can answer "what did you feel?" and "what did you do?" with accuracy, not defensiveness
They accept comfort without requiring indulgence — they allow the adult's presence to help them calm down without demanding that the situation or consequence be changed
They participate in daily feelings check-ins with genuine answers, not rote responses
They can identify at least one tool from their calm-down toolbox and describe when they used it

narration_prompts.prompt: What is a feeling you had this week that was really big?
narration_prompts.detail: What did it feel like in your body? What word would you give it?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between feeling angry and acting angry?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you feel angry without doing something angry?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you paused before reacting.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you do in the pause? What happened after?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between frustrated and furious?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you think of a time you were each one?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is in your calm-down toolbox?
narration_prompts.detail: What works best for you when a big feeling comes?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a character from a story who had a big feeling.
narration_prompts.detail: What did they feel? What did they do? Would you have done the same thing?

cross_connections.description: The stopping, waiting, and tolerating discomfort practiced in IC1.1-A1 are the behavioral precursors to emotional regulation. IC3.1-A formalizes the emotional awareness that IC1.1-A1 introduces through daily life. Both run concurrently in Phase 2.

IC3.2 teaches the body side of what IC3.1 teaches on the emotion side. The breathing, the physical calm-down practices, and the awareness of body signals are the physiological partner to emotional naming and regulation.
IC3.3 develops the ability to interpret events constructively. IC3.1 provides the emotional regulation that makes reframing possible — a child flooded by emotion cannot reframe anything.
The post-storm reflection practice is the earliest form of emotional self-examination, which IC1.6 will develop into a deeper interior life practice.

Emotional regulation is the foundation of relational competence. The child who can name their feelings and use words instead of outbursts can navigate friendships and family relationships in ways that the emotionally reactive child cannot.

content.why_this_matters:
You have feelings. Big ones. They come fast and they come strong — anger that floods your whole body, sadness that makes everything feel heavy, excitement that makes you want to jump and shout, fear that makes you want to hide. These feelings are real. They are not bad. They are not wrong. They are signals — messages from inside you about what is happening around you and to you.

But here is the thing most people never learn, even as adults: **a feeling is not a command.** Just because you feel angry does not mean you have to yell. Just because you feel scared does not mean you have to run. Just because you feel frustrated does not mean you have to throw something. The feeling is real. What you do about it is a choice.

Right now, when a big feeling comes, it probably feels like you and the feeling are the same thing. You do not feel angry — you become anger. You do not feel sad — you become sadness. The feeling takes over, and for a moment, there is no you separate from it. That is normal for your age. But this packet begins to change that. Slowly, over months and years, you will learn to feel a feeling — feel it fully, not push it away — and still choose what you do next.

That is one of the most powerful things a person can learn. The person who can feel strong emotions and still choose their response cannot be controlled by anyone who tries to use feelings as a weapon. That person is free in a way that most people are not.

You are going to start building that freedom now. Not by learning to stop feeling. By learning to feel and still think. Feel and still choose. Feel and still act the way the person you want to be would act.
content.what_this_develops:
This packet does not produce a child who never gets upset. That child does not exist, and if they did, something would be wrong. This packet develops:

1. **Naming emotions accurately** — not just "mad" and "sad," but the real words: frustrated, embarrassed, disappointed, scared, overwhelmed, jealous, lonely, excited, proud, worried
2. **Understanding that feelings pass** — the feeling is real and temporary; both things are true at the same time
3. **Pausing before reacting** — the physical habit of stopping between the feeling and the action, even for a moment
4. **Using words instead of outbursts** — learning that expression is the alternative to explosion
5. **Receiving comfort without being indulged** — the adult offers presence and calm without amplifying the emotion or eliminating the natural consequence
6. **Beginning to reflect** — after the feeling passes, the ability to look back and say what happened, what they felt, and what they did
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Feelings Are Signals, Not Bosses

Imagine you have a dashboard inside you, like the dashboard of a car. When the car is low on fuel, a light comes on. The light does not drive the car to the gas station. It just tells the driver something important: you are running low. The driver decides what to do.

Your feelings work the same way. When you feel angry, that is a signal. It is telling you: something happened that you did not like, or something feels unfair, or someone crossed a line. The anger is information. It is not a boss that tells you what to do. You — the real you, the one behind the feeling — get to decide what happens next.

When you feel scared, that is a signal too. It is telling you: something feels dangerous, or unknown, or too big. The fear is useful — it is making you pay attention. But the fear does not get to decide whether you run, or freeze, or face the thing. You decide.

**The feeling is real. The feeling is not in charge.**

This is hard. It is hard for grown-ups too. Many adults never learn this. They spend their entire lives being pushed around by their feelings — angry, so they yell; scared, so they hide; embarrassed, so they lash out. They never learned that the feeling and the response are two different things.

You are learning it now.

### Part 2: The Feelings Dictionary — More Than Mad, Sad, Glad

Most people use about four words for their feelings: mad, sad, happy, scared. But there are many, many more feelings than four, and the more precisely you can name what you feel, the less power it has to overwhelm you. A scientist who can identify exactly what a thing is has more power over it than a person who calls everything "that stuff."

**Here are some feelings to learn:**

**In the "angry" family:**
- **Frustrated** — you are trying to do something and it is not working
- **Irritated** — something small is bothering you, like a mosquito bite on your patience
- **Furious** — the biggest anger; everything feels like it is on fire inside
- **Annoyed** — something is not how you want it, and it keeps happening

**In the "sad" family:**
- **Disappointed** — you expected something good and it did not happen
- **Lonely** — you want to be with someone and you are not, or you feel like no one understands
- **Hurt** — someone did or said something that made your heart ache
- **Homesick** — you miss a place or a person and want to be with them

**In the "scared" family:**
- **Worried** — you keep thinking about something bad that might happen
- **Nervous** — you are about to do something and your stomach feels fluttery
- **Overwhelmed** — everything feels like too much; like there is more coming at you than you can handle
- **Startled** — something surprised you and your body jumped before your brain caught up

**In the "embarrassed" family:**
- **Embarrassed** — you did something in front of people and you wish they had not seen it
- **Ashamed** — you did something you know was wrong and the feeling sits heavy
- **Self-conscious** — you feel like everyone is looking at you and judging

**Practice:** When you feel something strong, try to find the exact right word. Not just "I'm mad." Are you frustrated? Annoyed? Furious? Hurt? The moment you find the right word, something changes. The feeling does not disappear, but it gets smaller, more manageable. You named it, so now it is a thing you can look at instead of a thing that swallowed you.

Your mentor will help you practice this. When they see you upset, they may ask: "What are you feeling right now? Can you find the word?" This is not to annoy you. It is training. You are building a vocabulary for your inner life, and that vocabulary is a tool you will use for the rest of your life.

### Part 3: Feelings Pass

Here is one of the most important truths about feelings: **they end.**

Every single feeling you have ever had — the biggest anger, the deepest sadness, the most overwhelming fear — ended. It passed. The feeling was real while it was happening, and then it was not happening anymore.

This matters because when you are inside a big feeling, it feels like it will last forever. When you are furious, it feels like you will be furious for the rest of your life. When you are heartbroken, it feels like the sadness will never stop. But it always stops. Always. The feeling rises like a wave, it crests, and then it falls.

**The wave image:** Think of your feelings like waves at the beach. A big wave comes — it is powerful, it is loud, it pushes you around. But if you stand firm and let it pass, the water goes back out. Another wave will come. It will also pass. You do not have to fight the wave. You do not have to pretend it is not there. You just have to stay standing while it passes.

This does not mean you should ignore feelings or pretend they do not exist. It means you can feel the feeling fully — let it be as big and strong as it is — and know, at the same time, that it will pass. Both things are true. The feeling is real. The feeling is temporary.

**What helps while you wait for a feeling to pass:**
- Breathe. Slowly. In through your nose, out through your mouth. Three times.
- Press your feet into the floor and feel the ground under you.
- Name the feeling: "I am frustrated right now."
- Tell yourself: "This feeling is real, and it will pass."
- Find something to do with your hands — squeeze a ball, draw, hold something cool.

### Part 4: The Pause — The Most Important Moment

Between the feeling and the action, there is a moment. It might be a very small moment — so small you almost cannot find it. But it is there. And in that moment, you have a choice.

**Feeling → [PAUSE] → Action**

Without the pause, the pattern is automatic:
- Feel angry → hit
- Feel frustrated → yell
- Feel embarrassed → run away or lash out
- Feel scared → cry or freeze

With the pause, even a tiny one, something different becomes possible:
- Feel angry → [pause] → use words to say what is wrong
- Feel frustrated → [pause] → ask for help or try a different way
- Feel embarrassed → [pause] → take a breath and stay present
- Feel scared → [pause] → name the fear and decide what to do about it

**How to build the pause:**

The pause is a physical skill, not a thinking skill. You do not build it by understanding it. You build it by practicing it with your body.

1. **Stop.** When a big feeling hits, stop moving. Freeze your body. You already know how to do this from freeze dance and red light/green light — this is the same skill used in a real moment.
2. **Breathe.** One breath. That is all. One slow breath in through the nose, out through the mouth. That one breath is the pause.
3. **Name.** Say the feeling, out loud or in your head. "I am angry." The naming activates a different part of your brain — the thinking part. When the thinking part turns on, the reacting part loses a little of its grip.

That is it. Stop. Breathe. Name. Three things that take about five seconds. Those five seconds are the difference between reacting and responding. Between being controlled by the feeling and being a person who feels the feeling and chooses what to do.

You will not be able to do this every time. You will forget. You will get flooded by a feeling that is too fast and too big, and you will react before you can pause. That is normal. The goal is not perfection — it is practice. Every time you manage to pause, even once, the capacity grows. The pause gets a little easier to find the next time.

### Part 5: Words Instead of Outbursts

When a big feeling comes and you manage to pause, you need something to do with all that energy. The feeling is still there — you did not make it disappear. You just stopped it from controlling your body. Now what?

**You use words.**

Words are the alternative to explosion. When you can say "I am really frustrated because I cannot get this to work," you have done something remarkable — you have taken a feeling that was in your body and moved it into language. Language is something you can share with another person. Language is something that can be heard and responded to. An outburst — throwing, hitting, screaming — cannot be heard. It can only be survived.

**Practice sentences:**
- "I am angry because ___."
- "I feel frustrated when ___."
- "I am sad because ___."
- "I do not like it when ___."
- "I need help because I feel ___."
- "Can I have a minute? I am really upset right now."

That last one is important. Sometimes you are too upset to use words well. In that case, the best words are the ones that ask for space: "I need a minute." Then you take the minute — breathe, move, squeeze something — and when the wave has passed enough that you can think, you use the other words.

**This is not about being polite.** This is about being effective. The child who can say "I am really angry because you broke my thing" is more likely to be heard and to get a real response than the child who screams and throws something. Words work. Outbursts do not.

### Part 6: Comfort Without Indulgence

When you are upset, the adults around you will offer comfort. This is right — you are a child, and the presence of a calm adult helps your body learn to calm down. This is called co-regulation, and it is one of the most important things that happens between a child and the adults who care for them.

But comfort and indulgence are not the same thing.

**Comfort** is: the adult is present, calm, and kind. They acknowledge your feeling. "I can see you are really disappointed." They stay with you while the feeling passes. They do not tell you to stop feeling it. They do not rush you. They are a steady presence while the storm moves through.

**Indulgence** is: the adult removes the thing that caused the feeling so you do not have to feel it. You are disappointed because you cannot have the cookie — and the adult gives you the cookie to stop the crying. You are frustrated because you have to do your chore — and the adult does the chore for you. You are angry because of a consequence — and the adult removes the consequence.

Comfort says: "I am here with you while this is hard." Indulgence says: "Let me make the hard thing go away."

Comfort builds strength. Indulgence builds fragility.

You need comfort. You do not need indulgence. And over time, you will need less and less comfort from outside, because the calm that the adult's presence teaches your body will become your own calm. That is the entire arc of IC3.1 — from needing someone else to help you regulate, to regulating yourself.

### Part 7: After the Storm — Simple Reflection

After a big feeling has passed — not during, but after, when you are calm — there is something valuable to do: look back.

This is not punishment. It is not "let's talk about what you did wrong." It is four simple questions:

1. **What happened?** (The facts. Not whose fault it was. Just what happened.)
2. **What did you feel?** (The feeling word. As exact as you can get.)
3. **What did you do?** (The action you took. Honest — not what you wish you had done.)
4. **What would you do differently?** (If you could go back to that moment, knowing what you know now, what would you change?)

That is it. Four questions. No lecture. No extended conversation. You answer, the adult listens, and you move on.

The purpose is not to make you feel bad. The purpose is to build the habit of self-examination — looking at your own behavior honestly. Right now this happens with an adult guiding you through it. By the time you reach Weathering Storms, you will be able to do this on your own. By Standing Ground, it will be automatic — a constant, quiet awareness of your own emotional patterns.
content.formative_practices:
These are not one-time activities. They are ongoing daily and weekly practices that form the emotional awareness IC3.1 develops.

### Practice 1: Name It in Real Time [Daily, As Moments Arise]
When you feel something strong, name it. Out loud if possible. "I am frustrated." "I feel scared." "I am disappointed." Your mentor will help you with this — when they see you experiencing a strong emotion, they will ask you to name it. Over time, you will name it without being asked. The naming is the skill.

### Practice 2: The Pause Practice [Daily, As Moments Arise]
When a big feeling comes, practice Stop-Breathe-Name before acting. Stop your body. Take one slow breath. Name the feeling. Then choose your response. You will not catch every moment. You do not need to. Every time you manage to pause — even once in a whole day — the capacity is growing.

### Practice 3: Feelings Check-In [Daily, Morning or Evening]
Once a day, with your mentor, take 30 seconds to check in. "How are you feeling right now?" Not "fine" — a real word. "I feel calm." "I feel a little worried about tomorrow." "I feel happy because we played outside." This is vocabulary practice and self-awareness practice combined.

### Practice 4: The Wave Ride [As Needed]
When a big feeling comes and you cannot do anything about the situation — you just have to wait for the feeling to pass — practice riding the wave. Breathe. Feel your feet on the floor. Name the feeling. Wait. Notice when it starts to get smaller. You may want to tell your mentor afterward: "I had a big wave of anger and I rode it out." That recognition is progress.

### Practice 5: Post-Storm Reflection [After Emotional Incidents]
After any incident where a big feeling led to a reaction — good or bad — do the four-question reflection with your mentor. What happened? What did you feel? What did you do? What would you do differently? Keep it short. Keep it honest. No lecture. This is reflection, not correction.

### Practice 6: Feelings Stories [Weekly]
Once a week, read or hear a story where a character experiences a strong emotion. After the story, discuss: What did the character feel? How do you know? What did they do about it? Was that a good choice? What else could they have done? This builds emotional vocabulary and the habit of thinking about feelings as something separate from actions.

### Practice 7: Calm-Down Toolbox [Build Once, Use Ongoing]
Together with your mentor, build a personal calm-down toolbox — a set of things that help you when a big feeling comes. This might include: a breathing exercise, a squeeze ball, a favorite spot to sit, drawing, counting to ten, pressing your feet into the floor. The toolbox is yours. You choose what goes in it. The point is that when the wave comes, you have something to reach for besides the automatic reaction.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Your Calm Is the Curriculum

When the child is in the grip of a strong emotion — rage, terror, overwhelming frustration — your job is to be the steady thing in the room. Not cold. Not detached. Not performing calmness while seething inside. Actually regulated. Present, warm, and steady.

**What this looks like:**
- Your voice stays at a normal volume and pace. When everyone else speeds up, you slow down.
- Your body is open and grounded — not tense, not looming, not retreating.
- You name what you see without judgment: "I can see you are very frustrated right now."
- You do not match the child's emotional energy. You do not meet anger with anger or panic with panic.
- You do not rush to fix, lecture, or explain. You are present first. Everything else comes after the child can hear you — and they cannot hear you while they are flooded.

**What this does NOT look like:**
- Yelling "CALM DOWN!" (The irony is obvious but somehow universally practiced.)
- Mocking the emotion: "You're crying about THAT?"
- Dismissing: "You're fine. It's not a big deal."
- Threatening: "If you don't stop crying right now..."
- Matching: becoming just as upset as the child, which teaches the child that the adult cannot handle their emotions either.

### The Co-Regulation Cycle

Here is what happens neurologically when you remain calm while a child is dysregulated:

1. The child is flooded. Their amygdala has hijacked the brain. The prefrontal cortex (the thinking, choosing part) is offline. They literally cannot think clearly, make good decisions, or hear your reasoning.
2. Your calm presence — your regulated nervous system — communicates safety. Not through words. Through your body, your tone, your face, your breathing.
3. The child's nervous system begins to mirror yours. Heart rate starts to come down. Breathing begins to slow. The flood recedes.
4. The prefrontal cortex comes back online. Now — and only now — the child can hear you, think about what happened, and engage in reflection.

**The critical implication:** Everything you want to say — the correction, the lesson, the discussion about what happened — must wait until step 4. Trying to reason with a child in step 1 is not just ineffective, it is counterproductive. It communicates that you do not understand what is happening, which increases the child's sense of being alone in the feeling, which increases dysregulation.

Connect first. Redirect second. Teach third.

### When You Are Not Calm

You will not always be calm. You are a human being with your own emotional states, your own fatigue, your own triggers. The child will find every one of them.

When you are not calm:
1. Name it to yourself: "I am getting angry right now."
2. If possible, take your own pause. "I need a moment. I will be right back." Step away for 30 seconds. Breathe.
3. If you cannot step away, slow down. Speak less. Speak slower. Drop your volume.
4. If you do lose your regulation — if you yell, if you say something you should not have — own it afterward. "I got angry and I raised my voice. That was not the right way to handle that. I am sorry." This models exactly what IC3.1 is teaching the child: you can feel strongly, act imperfectly, reflect honestly, and do better next time.

Your imperfect moments, handled with honesty, teach the child more than your perfect moments do. The child who sees an adult lose control and then own it and repair it is learning the full cycle of emotional regulation, including the part where you recover from failure.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Emotion Naming

**Developing well:** The child uses specific emotion words without prompting in at least some situations. They say "I'm frustrated" instead of "I'M MAD." They can distinguish between related emotions (disappointed vs. sad, worried vs. scared) when asked.

**Developing slowly:** The child uses only basic emotion words (mad, sad, happy) or no words at all — the emotion is expressed entirely through behavior. When asked "what are you feeling?" the answer is "I don't know" or "bad."

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling specific vocabulary yourself? Are you naming the child's emotions in real time? Are you offering choices ("are you frustrated or disappointed?") rather than demanding the child produce the word from nothing? Is the vocabulary building happening in calm moments, not only during crises?

### 2. The Pause

**Developing well:** The child demonstrates a pause between feeling and action in some situations. It may be brief — a half-second hesitation before they would previously have acted immediately. They may use one element of Stop-Breathe-Name even if they do not use all three. They are catching themselves at least some of the time.

**Developing slowly:** The child moves from feeling to action with no gap. Every strong emotion produces an immediate behavioral response. There is no evidence of a pause developing.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you practiced the pause in calm moments? Is the child seeing you pause in your own small emotional moments? Are your expectations age-appropriate? (A 4-year-old's "pause" might be a half-second hesitation. A 9-year-old's might be a full Stop-Breathe-Name. Both are progress.)

### 3. Words Instead of Outbursts

**Developing well:** The child increasingly uses verbal expression instead of physical or explosive reactions. "I am really angry about this" instead of throwing. "I do not like that" instead of hitting. The shift does not need to be complete — the trend is toward verbal expression.

**Developing slowly:** Physical outbursts remain the primary response to strong emotion. There is no increase in verbal expression over time.

**Diagnostic questions:** When the child does use words, is that acknowledged? ("You told me you were angry instead of throwing. That is important.") Is the environment safe for verbal expression of negative emotions? (If the child says "I'm angry at you" and gets punished for it, they will not use words.) Are you distinguishing between the expression of the feeling (acceptable) and the behavior (potentially not acceptable)?

### 4. Feelings Pass — Understanding

**Developing well:** The child can recognize, after the fact, that a feeling they had is no longer present. "I was really upset, but I feel better now." They show increasing tolerance during the wave — less panic that the feeling will last forever, more willingness to ride it out.

**Developing slowly:** Every emotional episode feels like a crisis that will never end. The child cannot retrospectively acknowledge that a feeling passed — it is either consuming them or it is completely gone with no connection between the two states.

### 5. Post-Storm Reflection

**Developing well:** The child participates in the four-question reflection with honesty and some specificity. They can name what they felt and what they did, even if their answers are simple. They are beginning to generate "what I would do differently" answers that are genuine rather than rote.

**Developing slowly:** The child refuses to reflect, gives rote answers ("I don't know," "nothing"), or becomes defensive when the reflection is attempted.

**How to improve:** Make the reflection shorter, not longer. Share your own reflection first. Make it a conversation, not an interrogation. If the child is not ready to reflect, try again later — timing matters more than consistency here.

### 6. Comfort Without Indulgence

**Developing well:** The child accepts comfort — allows the adult's presence and calm to help them regulate — without escalating demands for the situation to change. They may still ask for the thing they want, but when told no, they process the disappointment with the adult's support rather than escalating until the adult gives in.

**Developing slowly:** Every emotional display is an escalation strategy — the child has learned that sufficient distress produces capitulation. The crying intensifies until the boundary moves. The child cannot calm in the adult's presence because the adult's presence is not a source of comfort; it is a negotiation partner.

**The fix is in the adult:** If this pattern is established, it was trained by adult inconsistency. The boundary must hold. The comfort must continue. Both, simultaneously, every time, until the child's nervous system learns that the comfort is real and the boundary is real and they are not in competition.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Telling the Child to Stop Feeling
"Stop crying." "Don't be angry." "There's nothing to be scared of." These statements communicate that the feeling is wrong. The feeling is never wrong. The behavior might need to change, but the feeling is always valid information. Suppressed feelings do not go away — they go underground and express themselves in other ways: anxiety, aggression, withdrawal, physical symptoms. IC3.1 is explicitly not about suppressing emotions. It is about experiencing them fully and choosing the response.

### Emotional Processing as Entertainment
Some parenting approaches encourage extensive emotional processing — talking about feelings at length, drawing the feelings, making the feelings the center of attention. IC3.1 takes a different approach: name it, feel it, let it pass, reflect briefly, move on. Prolonged processing can actually amplify the emotion rather than resolving it. The child learns that emotions are dramatic events that require elaborate attention, rather than normal signals that arise and pass as part of ordinary life.

### Praising the Absence of Emotion
"You were so brave — you didn't even cry!" This teaches the child that not feeling (or not showing the feeling) is the goal. It is not. The goal is to feel fully and still choose well. The better praise: "You were really upset and you used your words. That took a lot of strength."

### Using Your Own Emotions as Leverage
"You're making Mommy sad." "Daddy is angry because you..." This teaches the child that they are responsible for the adult's emotional state — which is the exact opposite of IC3.1's lesson. Each person is responsible for their own emotional regulation. The child is responsible for their behavior, not for the adult's feelings about that behavior.

### Expecting Too Much Too Fast
Emotional regulation develops slowly over years. The 4-year-old who melts down is not failing. They are being 4. The 7-year-old who still has occasional outbursts is not failing. The question is always trajectory — is there improvement over months? If a child who had daily meltdowns at age 5 has weekly ones at age 7 and occasional ones at age 9, that is excellent progress, even if the occasional meltdown looks dramatic.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: A Big Feeling This Week
**Strong:** Names a specific emotion with some precision ("I was really frustrated when the Legos kept falling apart"). Can describe what it felt like in their body ("my face got hot," "my stomach felt tight"). Shows awareness that the feeling was a distinct experience.
**Weak:** "I don't know." "I was fine all week." (Probably not — every child has emotional experiences worth examining.)

### Prompt 2: Feeling vs. Acting
**Strong:** Can articulate, even simply, that the feeling and the action are different things. "I can feel angry and not hit." "Being mad is in my body, but hitting is what I choose to do."
**Weak:** Cannot separate the two. "If I'm mad I have to hit." The feeling and the action are experienced as one event.

### Prompt 3: A Time They Paused
**Strong:** Can recall a specific instance where they stopped before reacting. Describes what they did in the pause, even if it was small. Shows pride in the moment.
**Weak:** Cannot recall an instance — which may mean it has not happened yet, or may mean they are not yet aware of it when it does. The absence of a recalled pause is diagnostic information, not failure.

### Prompt 4: Frustrated vs. Furious
**Strong:** Can distinguish between the two with examples. "Frustrated is when something won't work. Furious is when I'm so angry I can barely think." The distinction is felt, not just defined.
**Weak:** "They're the same." The vocabulary is not yet differentiating the emotional landscape.

### Prompt 5: Calm-Down Toolbox
**Strong:** Names specific tools and can describe when they have used them. "I squeeze the stress ball when I'm angry. I breathe when I'm worried." Shows ownership of the toolbox.
**Weak:** Cannot name any tools, or names them but has never used them. The toolbox is theoretical rather than practiced.

### Prompt 6: Character in a Story
**Strong:** Names a character, identifies their feeling, and describes their choice. Can articulate that the character felt something and did something — two separate things. "Sophie was really angry and she went outside and climbed a tree until she felt better."
**Weak:** Cannot recall a story, or describes the character's actions without connecting them to feelings.
